context: Current closing price of Wabtec (WAB): $268.29 USD as of 2026-04-14.
justification_text:
My probabilistic forecast for the variable centered on 268.29 across all time horizons was driven by the assumption that the current value or a very recent average represented the most likely future outcome in the absence of strong evidence for a directional trend. This approach suggests a belief in the relative stability of the underlying system or market in the short to medium term. When a single point estimate is carried forward across different horizons, it often implies that the most immediate data point or a well-established mean is the best unbiased predictor, serving as a baseline expectation around which future observations are expected to fluctuate.

The varying widths of the 90% confidence intervals reflect the inherent increase in uncertainty as the forecast horizon extends. For the 1-day forecast, the interval was relatively narrow, [260.67, 276.01], indicating that we expected most daily fluctuations to stay within a tight range around the point estimate. As we looked out to 1 week, the interval widened significantly to [250.9, 285.58], acknowledging that more time allows for greater cumulative deviations. This trend continued dramatically for the 1-month forecast, with the interval expanding to [232.89, 303.62]. These widening intervals primarily account for the natural volatility and random noise inherent in the variable over longer periods, as well as any potential model imperfections in capturing longer-term dynamics, even if no specific directional trend was predicted.

While the 90% confidence intervals capture expected variability under normal circumstances, there are always specific risks or "tail events" that could push outcomes outside these stated ranges. These typically include unforeseen exogenous shocks or fundamental shifts that are not captured by historical data or the model's assumptions of normal distribution. Examples could include sudden and significant policy changes, major economic downturns or booms, unexpected geopolitical conflicts, technological disruptions, or even large-scale natural disasters. Such events introduce systemic risk and can lead to outcomes far removed from even the wider 1-month confidence interval, as they fundamentally alter the underlying conditions that the forecast implicitly assumes will remain largely consistent.
